section.title: The Voyage and the Shipwreck
section.sectionRationale: Luke tells the sea journey to Rome in vivid, eyewitness detail: a doomed voyage, a killer storm, the loss of all hope, and a shipwreck from which every soul is saved. Amid the terror the prisoner becomes the steadying center, because God has promised he will stand before Caesar. The chapter shows the sovereign faithfulness of God carrying His witness to Rome through the worst the sea can do.
section.thesis: When the storm strips away every human hope, it is the bound apostle who stands up with courage, for an angel of the God he serves has promised that not one life will be lost and that Paul must stand before Caesar; and so all two hundred and seventy-six come safely to land.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the voyage from Caesarea toward Italy; Paul's warning overruled at Fair Havens; the storm that drives them to abandon hope; Paul's word of courage grounded in God's promise; and the shipwreck on Malta where everyone is brought safely ashore.

verses.text: When it was decided that we would sail for Italy, Paul and some other prisoners were handed over to a centurion named Julius, who belonged to the Imperial Regiment.
We boarded an Adramyttian ship about to sail for ports along the coast of Asia, and we put out to sea. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, was with us.
The next day we landed at Sidon, and Julius treated Paul with consideration, allowing him to visit his friends and receive their care.
After putting out from there, we sailed to the lee of Cyprus because the winds were against us.
And when we had sailed across the open sea off the coast of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra in Lycia.
There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship sailing for Italy and put us on board.
After sailing slowly for many days, we arrived off Cnidus. When the wind impeded us, we sailed to the lee of Crete, opposite Salmone.
After we had moved along the coast with difficulty, we came to a place called Fair Havens, near the town of Lasea.

units.label: Paul's warning overruled
units.note: the majority decided to sail on

verses.text: By now much time had passed, and the voyage had already become dangerous because it was after the Fast. So Paul advised them,
“Men, I can see that our voyage will be filled with disaster and great loss, not only to ship and cargo, but to our own lives as well.”
But contrary to Paul’s advice, the centurion was persuaded by the pilot and by the owner of the ship.

Since the harbor was unsuitable to winter in, the majority decided to sail on, hoping that somehow they could reach Phoenix to winter there. Phoenix was a harbor in Crete facing both southwest and northwest.

units.label: The storm and all hope lost
units.note: we abandoned all hope of being saved

verses.text: When a gentle south wind began to blow, they thought they had their opportunity. So they weighed anchor and sailed along, hugging the coast of Crete.
But it was not long before a cyclone called the Northeaster swept down across the island.
Unable to head into the wind, the ship was caught up. So we gave way and let ourselves be driven along.
Passing to the lee of a small island called Cauda, we barely managed to secure the lifeboat.
After hoisting it up, the crew used ropes to undergird the ship. And fearing that they would run aground on the sandbars of Syrtis, they lowered the sea anchor and were driven along.
We were tossed so violently that the next day the men began to jettison the cargo.
On the third day, they threw the ship’s tackle overboard with their own hands.
When neither sun nor stars appeared for many days and the great storm continued to batter us, we abandoned all hope of being {{sozo|saved}}.

After the men had gone a long time without food, Paul stood up among them and said, “Men, you should have followed my advice not to sail from Crete. Then you would have averted this disaster and loss.
But now I urge you to keep up your {{euthymeo|courage}}, because you will not experience any loss of life, but only of the ship.
For just last night an {{angelos|angel}} of the God to whom I belong and whom I serve stood beside me
and said, ‘Do not be afraid, Paul; you must stand before Caesar. And look, God has granted you the lives of all who sail with you.’
So take courage, men, for I believe God that it will happen just as He told me.
However, we must run aground on some island.”

units.label: The shipwreck and the rescue
units.note: everyone was brought safely to land

verses.text: On the fourteenth night we were still being driven across the Adriatic Sea. About midnight the sailors sensed they were approaching land.
They took soundings and found that the water was twenty fathoms deep. Going a little farther, they took another set of soundings that read fifteen fathoms.
Fearing that we would run aground on the rocks, they dropped four anchors from the stern and prayed for daybreak.
Meanwhile, the sailors attempted to escape from the ship. Pretending to lower anchors from the bow, they let the lifeboat down into the sea.
But Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers, “Unless these men remain with the ship, you cannot be saved.”
So the soldiers cut the ropes to the lifeboat and set it adrift.
Right up to daybreak, Paul kept urging them all to eat: “Today is your fourteenth day in constant suspense, without taking any food.
So for your own preservation, I urge you to eat something, because not a single hair of your head will be lost.”
After he had said this, Paul took bread and {{eucharisteo|gave thanks}} to God in front of them all. Then he broke it and began to eat.
They were all encouraged and took some food themselves.
In all, there were 276 of us on board.
After the men had eaten their fill, they lightened the ship by throwing the grain into the sea.
When daylight came, they did not recognize the land, but they sighted a bay with a sandy beach, where they decided to run the ship aground if they could.
Cutting away the anchors, they left them in the sea as they loosened the ropes that held the rudders. Then they hoisted the foresail to the wind and made for the beach.
But the ship struck a sandbar and ran aground. The bow stuck fast and would not move, and the stern was being broken up by the pounding of the waves.
The soldiers planned to kill the prisoners so none of them could swim to freedom.
But the centurion, wanting to spare Paul’s life, thwarted their plan. He commanded those who could swim to jump overboard first and get to land.
The rest were to follow on planks and various parts of the ship. In this way everyone was brought safely to land.

sozo.gloss: To save, rescue, or bring safely through. Here it means rescue from the sea, yet it is the same word the New Testament uses for salvation from sin and death. When all hope of being saved is gone, the God who saves keeps every soul alive, a picture of the deliverance He works for those who trust Him.

euthymeo.gloss: To take heart, to be of good cheer or courage. Twice Paul urges the terrified crew to take courage, not by denying the danger but because God has spoken. Christian courage is not the absence of storms but confidence in the promise and character of God in the midst of them.

angelos.gloss: A messenger, an angel. An angel of the God whom Paul serves stands by him in the night with a word of assurance. Throughout Acts, God sends His messengers at decisive moments to guide, protect, and encourage His servants and to move the mission forward.

eucharisteo.gloss: To give thanks. Paul takes bread and gives thanks to God before all, then breaks it and eats. The language echoes the thanksgiving at meals and at the Lord's table; in the very teeth of the storm, the man of God gives thanks and steadies a shipload of frightened people.

basic.context: Paul, still a prisoner, is put aboard ship for the long journey to Rome, with Luke and Aristarchus alongside him. The season for safe sailing is closing. Paul warns against pressing on, but he is overruled, and a ferocious storm seizes the ship. For two weeks they are driven helplessly until all hope is gone. Then Paul, standing on God's promise, tells them not one life will be lost, and after a shipwreck on Malta everyone reaches shore alive.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this gripping travel narrative for what it reveals about God's providence and about Paul. Notice the reversals: the prisoner gives the orders that matter, the expert sailors are helpless, and the one who trusts God becomes the calm center. The hinge is verses 22 to 25, where God's promise turns despair into courage.
basic.originalLanguages: When they abandon all hope of being sozo, saved, from the sea, God saves them all. Paul twice tells them to euthymeo, take courage, because an angelos, an angel, of God has promised their lives. And at the darkest hour Paul takes bread and does eucharisteo, gives thanks, before them all.
basic.typology: The storm and rescue recall God's rule over the sea, the chaos He masters at creation and at the exodus, and Jesus who stilled the storm and walked on the waves. Paul, like his Lord, brings peace and preservation in the midst of the deep, a servant carrying the presence of the God who saves.
basic.christ: Paul mirrors his Master, who slept in a storm and then calmed it, and who saves His people through the flood rather than around it. The salvation of all on board, when every hope was lost, pictures the greater rescue in Christ, who brings His people safely through death itself to the far shore.
basic.god: God rules the wind and waves and keeps His word. He had promised Paul would reach Rome, and no storm can break that promise. He grants Paul the lives of all who sail with him, showing that His care for His servant spills over to spare many. The God who serves as Paul's anchor holds the whole ship.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 23:11 gave the promise that Paul must testify in Rome. Psalm 107 describes sailors in a storm crying to the Lord who stills it. Mark 4 shows Jesus calming the sea. Romans 8 declares that nothing, not even peril or sword, can separate us from God's purpose in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The risen Christ is bringing His witness to Rome, and not even a killer storm can stop the mission. God preserves Paul and all with him, driving the gospel onward toward the heart of the empire and the ends of the earth, exactly as He promised.

context.literary: This is one of the most detailed sea voyages in ancient literature and part of the we passages, where Luke writes as an eyewitness companion. The vivid nautical detail heightens the sense of real danger and real deliverance. Structurally it forms the penultimate stage of Paul's journey to Rome, showing God's faithful providence carrying the mission through catastrophe to its goal.
context.historical: Grain ships from Alexandria regularly carried passengers to Italy. Sailing after the Fast, around the Day of Atonement in early autumn, was dangerous, and after mid-November the sea was closed. The Northeaster, a violent wind off Crete, could drive a ship for days. Roman soldiers were liable with their own lives for escaped prisoners, which explains the plan to kill them.
context.canonical: The chapter fulfills the Lord's promise in Acts 23:11 that Paul would testify in Rome and displays the God of the Psalms who rules the raging sea, Psalm 107:23 to 30. It echoes the Gospels' storm scenes and anticipates the New Testament theme that God brings His people safely through, not merely around, the deepest dangers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the perilous journey to Rome as an eyewitness, to display God's sovereign faithfulness in keeping His promise to Paul, and to show the steadying power of faith that rests on God's word amid disaster.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a specific voyage and rescue; it does not promise that believers will always be spared physical harm. The abiding truths are God's providential rule, the reliability of His promises, and the courage and usefulness that faith brings to others in a crisis.

debates.question: Is Paul's breaking of bread with thanksgiving in verse 35 an ordinary meal or the Lord's Supper?

views.label: An ordinary meal of thanksgiving
views.case: The setting is a starving, mostly pagan crew who need food to survive the coming swim; Paul urges them to eat for their preservation, verse 34, and gives thanks as any devout Jew would before a meal, so the natural reading is a normal meal eaten before God, not a sacrament shared with unbelievers.

views.label: A deliberate echo of the Lord's Supper
views.case: Luke uses the same fourfold language, took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and ate, that marks the Last Supper and the Emmaus meal, Luke 22:19 and 24:30; on this view Paul consciously turns a common meal into an act of worship and witness that points the whole ship to the God who saves.

debates.bearing: Whether or not it is the Supper, the point stands: in the darkest hour the man of God gives public thanks and so encourages everyone. The debate guards against both emptying the scene of its worshipful resonance and reading a full sacrament into a meal shared with a pagan crew; either way Paul bears witness to God through open thanksgiving.

study.originalLanguages: The storm story is shot through with the language of rescue and faith. When the crew give up all hope of being sozo, saved, from the sea, it is God who saves them, the same verb Scripture uses for salvation from sin. Paul twice urges them to euthymeo, take courage, not by pretending the danger away but because God has spoken through an angelos, an angel, who stood by him in the night. And at the crisis Paul takes bread and does eucharisteo, gives thanks, before them all, steadying the ship by an act of open trust in God.

typology.type: The Lord who rules the raging sea
typology.fulfillment: The God who masters wind and wave in the Psalms and stills the storm in the Gospels is the same God who preserves this ship, fulfilled in Christ who commands the sea, Mark 4:39.
As Noah and his household were brought safely through the waters, so all on board are saved through the storm, a shadow of the salvation God works in Christ, 1 Peter 3:20 to 21.

typology.type: The servant who brings peace in the storm
typology.fulfillment: Paul, calm and encouraging amid terror, follows his Master who slept in the storm and then spoke peace, pointing to Christ our peace in every trial, John 16:33.

study.christ: Though Christ is not named in the storm, He is present in it. Paul serves the God who sends the angel, and that God is the Father of the Lord Jesus. Paul embodies his Master, who was untroubled in the storm and saved His people through it. The rescue of every soul when all hope was lost is a living picture of the salvation Christ works, bringing His people safely through death to the shore of the world to come. The thanksgiving over broken bread points, at the very least, to the God who feeds and saves in Christ.
study.god: God is the true captain of this voyage. He rules the wind and the sea, keeps His promise that Paul will reach Rome, and grants him the lives of all aboard. His providence works through ordinary means, seamanship, soldiers, planks, and through His direct word by the angel. He does not spare His servant the storm, but He carries him through it, and many others with him, proving Himself faithful and mighty to save.

crossReferences.note: The Lord's promise that Paul must testify in Rome, the guarantee that no storm can sink the ship.
Sailors in a tempest cry to the Lord, who stills the storm and brings them to their desired haven.
Jesus asleep in the storm, then rising to command the wind and sea to be still.
Neither peril nor sword can separate God's people from His love and purpose in Christ.

study.oneStory: The ascended Christ is steering His witness to Rome, and the sea itself must serve that plan. When human skill fails and hope dies, God saves everyone on board and keeps His promise, driving the gospel onward toward the capital of the world. The storm cannot drown the mission; the God who saves brings His servant safely through to the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: On what basis did Paul tell the terrified crew to take courage?

options.text: God had sent an angel promising that not one life would be lost and that Paul must stand before Caesar.
options.note: Paul grounds his courage in God's specific promise delivered by the angel, verses 22 to 24.

options.text: He judged that the storm was beginning to weaken.
options.note: The storm had not weakened; the courage rested on God's word, not on improving weather.

options.text: He trusted the skill of the experienced sailors.
options.note: The sailors were helpless and even tried to flee; the hope was in God, not in seamanship.

options.text: He had no real reason and was simply trying to calm them.
options.note: He had a firm reason: a direct promise from God, which he believed would come true.

quiz.q: How does the chapter show God's providence at work?

options.text: God keeps His promise that Paul will reach Rome and preserves every life on board.
options.note: The promise of Acts 23:11 holds, and all 276 come safely to land, verses 24 and 44.

options.text: God calms the storm as soon as Paul prays.
options.note: God does deliver them, but not by instantly stilling the storm; they endure it and are shipwrecked, yet all are saved.

options.text: God rewards the crew for listening to Paul's first warning.
options.note: They did not listen to his warning; they sailed against his advice, yet God still spared them.

options.text: God abandons the ship because of the sailors' unbelief.
options.note: The opposite; God preserves everyone, believer and unbeliever alike, for His servant's sake.

quiz.q: What happened to those on board by the end of the chapter?

options.text: The ship was destroyed, but everyone was brought safely to land.
options.note: Paul had said they would lose the ship but not their lives, and so it happened, verses 22 and 44.

options.text: The soldiers spared the prisoners because the centurion wanted to save Paul.
options.note: True, verse 43, but the fuller outcome is that all 276 reached shore alive.

options.text: Most were lost, and only Paul survived.
options.note: No one was lost; the whole company was saved, just as God had promised.

options.text: They reached the harbor of Phoenix safely as planned.
options.note: They never reached Phoenix; the storm drove them to shipwreck on Malta.

quiz.q: Why did Paul insist the sailors stay with the ship in verse 31?

options.text: Because God's promise of rescue would be worked out through them remaining, not by abandoning the ship.
options.note: God's certain promise did not cancel the means; without the sailors none could be saved, so Paul held them to their duty.

options.text: Because the lifeboat was too small for everyone.
options.note: There was a practical dimension, but Paul's stated reason ties their staying to being saved, the outworking of God's promise.

options.text: Because he wanted to punish the sailors for trying to flee.
options.note: It was not about punishment but about the means God would use to save them all.

options.text: Because he doubted God would really rescue them.
options.note: Paul fully believed God's promise; that is exactly why he acted to secure the means of its fulfillment.

quiz.q: What did Paul do at the darkest point to encourage everyone?

options.text: He took bread, gave thanks to God before them all, and urged them to eat.
options.note: Paul's public thanksgiving and call to eat lifted the whole company, verses 33 to 36.

options.text: He reminded them they should have heeded his earlier warning.
options.note: He did say this, verse 21, but what actually encouraged them was his thanksgiving and the promise of survival.

options.text: He kept silent so as not to alarm them further.
options.note: Paul did not stay silent; he spoke up repeatedly with courage and thanksgiving.

options.text: He demanded the soldiers release him first.
options.note: He sought no privilege for himself; he served the whole company's good.

questions: Paul stayed calm and useful because he trusted a specific promise of God. What promise of God do you most need to lean on when life feels out of control?
The prisoner became the steadying center of a panicking ship. How might God want to use your faith to bring courage to others in a crisis?
God did not spare Paul the storm, but He carried him through it. How does that reshape what you expect from God in the middle of hardship?
Paul gave public thanks to God before a frightened, unbelieving crew. Where could a simple act of gratitude become a quiet witness in your daily life?
God granted Paul the lives of all who sailed with him. Whose good might God intend to bless through His faithfulness to you?

translationNotes.note: The Fast refers to the Day of Atonement, Yom Kippur, in early autumn; sailing after it was hazardous, and by mid-November the sea lanes closed for winter. Paul's warning reflects the real danger of the season.
The Adriatic Sea in Luke's usage covered a wider area than today, including the central Mediterranean south of Italy, the waters through which the ship was being driven.
The number aboard is given as 276, though a few manuscripts read about 76. The larger number, well attested, underscores the scale of the rescue in which every single person was saved.
